retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Invagination -- Evaluation -- BI is most often diagnosed pertaining to the following midsagittal craniovertebral parameters:
retrieved_docs.content: Chamberlain's line-running from the posterior aspect of the hard palate and the posterior margin of the foramen magnum McGregor's line-running from the posterior margin of the hard palate to the lowest aspect of the squama occipitalis (as the posterior edge of the foramen magnum could not always be visualized) McRae's line – connotes the anteroposterior line at the foramen magnum [15] [17] The criteria for diagnosis are the tip of the dens lying >5 mm above Chamberlain's Line, >7 mm above McGregor's Line, or McRae's line.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Advantages and Disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: Conservative access cavity Focuses on preserving the pericervical dentin and part of the pulp chamber roof, while allowing adequate canal access. Advantages: Enhances tooth strength compared to TradAC. Disadvantages: Slightly limited visual access may be required for effective cleaning, which may necessitate the use of magnification. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The UltraAC is indicated for anterior teeth with intact crowns and minimal structural loss, particularly when preoperative imaging confirms a single straight canal. These cases often benefit from the use of high magnification or guided access methods to maintain precision while preserving as much tooth structure as possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – Morphologically, the primary maxillary second molars (tooth A, J) resemble the permanent maxillary first molar. Rhomboidal in shape, the maxillary second molar has four cusps, two on both the buccal and lingual aspects. A fifth cusp can be present and is called the tubercle of Carabelli. Two well-defined cusps can be seen on the buccal view, and the lingual surface has three cusps: the mesiolingual, distolingual, and the cusp of Carabelli.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional technique complications
retrieved_docs.content: Craniocervical perforation may develop when the convenience extension is left unfinished before the bur shaft enters the canal space. [63] [61] [62] Pulp debris left behind during preparation may also discolor the crown, especially when the access cavity lacks proper incisal extension and lies too close to the gingival margin. [63] [62] [40] Misjudgment of the tooth's lingual-axial or mesial-axial inclination can result in abrasion of the labial or distal wall during instrumentation. [63] [61] [62] Failure to complete the convenience extension can create a ledge at the apical-labial curve, where the instrument shaft rests on the shoulder or cavity edge instead of advancing smoothly into the canal. [63] [61] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative CBCT before tooth extraction is mainly related to impacted mandibular third molars due to the risk of injuring the inferior alveolar nerve (IAN) during surgery. [8] However, the radiograph of choice before removing mandibular third molars is orthopantomography. This x-ray will provide information about the proximity of the tooth with the IAN. The most common radiographic signs associated with IAN injury are the darkening of the roots of the third molar, interruption of the cortical lines of the canal, and diversion of the canal. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Extraction of Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: CBCT imaging must only be indicated in specific cases where the operator has a clinical question that panoramic or intraoral radiographs cannot answer. [17] Up-to-date data shows that using CBCT before removing mandibular third molars does not reduce nerve injury or lead to better patient outcomes than panoramic radiographs. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose Paranasal Sinuses -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Sinus
retrieved_docs.content: The orbit floor forms the superior wall, the sinus roof. The infraorbital artery (branch of the maxillary artery) and nerve (branch of the maxillary division of the trigeminal nerve) run through this wall and enter the infraorbital groove. [9] The medial wall separates the maxillary sinus from the nasal cavity. Still, they communicate throughout the ostium, located in the medial wall inferior or at the same level of the orbit floor. [9] The inferior wall, known as the sinus floor, is closely related to the posterior teeth apices, from which it is separated only by a layer of compact bone. The mean distance between the dental apices and the sinus floor is 1.97 mm, [10] and the apices of the molars are closer to the sinus floor than the premolars. [10] The first molars have been found to perforate the sinus floor in 2.2% of cases, and the second molars in 2% of cases. The buccodistal root of the second molar is the closest to the maxillary sinus floor. [2] The maxillary sinuses frequently present septa, thin plates of cortical bone arising from the sinus floor, better visualized on cone-beam CT scans. Primary septa occur during the development of the sinus [11] , whereas secondary septa occur after tooth loss. [12] Apart from septa, tooth loss causes a local lowering of the sinus floor and alveolar bone resorption. [9] Mucus-producing ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium lines the maxillary sinus, which lacks periosteum. The cilia have the heavy task of draining the mucous into the ostium (located superiorly in the medial wall), and they are more numerous near the ostium. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The TrussAC is recommended when maintaining structural integrity is paramount, yet entry into multiple canals is required. This approach is most applicable in situations where canal orientations allow separate, small entry points and where restorative longevity and functional durability are key considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The maxillary first molars (tooth B, I) morphologically resemble both a molar and a premolar. Its occlusal surface consists of the mesiobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and distobuccal cusps. The largest cusp is the mesiolingual cusp, and it also represents the sharpest cusp. The distolingual cusp is small and rounded in morphology. The buccal surface is smooth, and the three roots are long and widely spread. Of note, the primary 1st molar has a less pronounced mesiobuccal cusp when compared to the permanent 1st molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The ConsAC is best suited for vital or minimally compromised anterior teeth, where preserving pericervical dentin is a priority. ConsAC is typically chosen for teeth with straightforward canal anatomy and minimal calcification, particularly when long-term fracture resistance is a central treatment goal.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral canals can be present in different numbers in a certain tooth and cause problems during endodontic or root therapies. Radiopaque materials can be used to indicate the number and position of these canals.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The mandibular first molars (tooth S, L) have four cusps: two buccal and two lingual. These include the distobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and mesiobuccal cusps. The occlusal surface is narrow secondary to mesiobuccal and mesiolingual cusp convergence. There are three pits.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Advantages and Disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: Access cavity designs in anterior teeth have evolved from conventional methods to minimally invasive techniques. The main classifications include:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: The cervical canal structure is anatomically situated within the structure of the internal os, with the internal os serving as the parent structure that encompasses the cervical canal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2055
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the eye are classified as a subcategory of histopathology procedures that are not placed in any other specific category, indicating that eye‑related diagnostic methods fall under the broader, uncategorized group of histopathological examinations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: The head of the first metatarsal bone is a distinct anatomical region of the foot that is defined by laterality, indicating that it can be identified as belonging to either the left or right side.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1510
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid ophthalmologic and otologic preparations are a specific type of ophthalmological and otological preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 833
retrieved_docs.content: Ophthalmologic and otologic diagnosis and treatment is described as classifying itself, indicating that the domain of ophthalmologic and otologic diagnosis and treatment serves as its own categorical framework for organizing related diagnostic and therapeutic procedures within the same specialty.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.
